narrative:
As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical 3.21 average—and a strong LSAT score of 37, slightly above the dataset average of 36.70, you've built an impressive academic foundation that many law school hopefuls would envy. Your family comes from a middle-income bracket, which is common among applicants, and your Year 3 school decile ranking of 7th places you well above the average of 5.53, showing clear improvement over time. However, your Year 1 decile of 4th sits below the average of 5.71, reflecting a slower start in your early grades. The university's admission model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on profiles like yours from past students, estimated a 64% chance that you would fail the bar exam. This high predicted failure risk led directly to the rejection of your application, as the school prioritizes admitting those with the strongest projected odds of passing on the first try.

The most influential factor pushing the model toward predicting bar failure for you was your plan to attend part-time, a choice only about 7% of students make compared to the overwhelming 93% who go full-time. Being part-time stood out as the top driver, significantly increasing the model's concern about your bar success, likely because historical data shows it correlates with tougher odds. Next in importance was your strong Year 3 decile ranking, which worked strongly in your favor by pulling the prediction toward expecting you to pass, highlighting your upward trajectory as a positive signal. Rounding out the top three was your lower Year 1 decile, which nudged the prediction toward failure by underscoring that early academic stumble relative to peers.

Compared to typical students in the dataset, your profile is stronger in later performance and test scores but held back by the part-time status and initial grades, tipping the overall balance against you. While this decision stings, especially given your improvements and background, it reflects the model's data-driven focus on bar passage patterns rather than your full potential. To move forward, consider reapplying after bolstering your early-grade narrative—perhaps through additional coursework or a full-time commitment plan—and target schools with more flexible models for part-time students. You could also retake the LSAT for an even higher edge or gather letters emphasizing your growth since Year 1. With your upward trend, persistence could turn this into just a detour on your path to law school.
